heroOverview: A perineocele is a herniation of pelvic contents — usually small bowel or peritoneal fat — through a weakened or absent perineal body, creating a distinct posterior vaginal bulge that occupies the space between the rectum and the anus rather than at the more typical rectovaginal or apical levels. It is one of the least-recognised compartments of posterior pelvic floor prolapse, frequently misclassified as a rectocele or enterocele on clinical examination alone and distinguished reliably only by dynamic defaecography or pelvic floor MRI. Perineocele is almost exclusively a condition of women who have sustained obstetric perineal injury, and it commonly coexists with rectocele, enterocele, or fecal incontinence from underlying sphincter disruption. Reconstruction of the perineal body — either by a urogynecologist or a colorectal pelvic floor surgeon — is the definitive treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact prevalence is unknown due to frequent misclassification; posterior perineal hernias represent approximately 5–10% of posterior-compartment prolapse cases referred for defaecography
keyStats.demographics: Exclusively or near-exclusively affects women; peak presentation in the fifth to seventh decades of life following earlier obstetric perineal trauma
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed between ages 45 and 70
keyStats.globalCases: Considered underdiagnosed; a 2019 review estimated that perineocele may account for 5–15% of symptomatic posterior pelvic organ prolapse cases when defaecography is used systematically

definition: A perineocele (ICD-10: N81.81) is a form of posterior pelvic organ prolapse in which pelvic contents herniate through the perineal body — the fibromuscular pyramid of tissue located between the posterior vaginal wall, the external anal sphincter, and the perineal skin. Unlike a rectocele, which represents the rectal wall bulging into the mid-vagina through a defect in the rectovaginal fascia, a perineocele occurs at a lower level, between the rectum and the anal canal, and typically involves descent of small bowel, omentum, or peritoneal fat through an attenuated or avulsed perineal body rather than a rectal wall herniation. The condition falls within the specialist domain of urogynecology and female pelvic medicine, often requiring collaborative input from colorectal surgery when anorectal dysfunction accompanies the structural defect. It is graded using the pelvic organ prolapse quantification (POP-Q) system alongside thorough imaging characterisation. Perineoceles are clinically important because they are a structural substrate for obstructed defecation and, in severe cases, fecal incontinence, yet they respond poorly to standard rectocele repair if the diagnosis is not established preoperatively. The perineal body integrates the central tendons of the perineum and serves as the critical anchor for the external anal sphincter, the bulbospongiosus, and the transverse perineal muscles; its disruption compromises all these structures simultaneously.

typesClassification.description: A defect confined to the perineal body in which the perineal skin descends between the anus and the vaginal opening without an associated rectocele or enterocele component. This is the most specific form and requires targeted perineorrhaphy.

The most common presentation: the perineal body and the rectovaginal fascia are both compromised, producing a combined posterior and perineal bulge. Both defects require simultaneous repair for adequate outcome.
Small bowel or peritoneal fat herniates through the weakened perineal body, often visible as a soft bowel-containing mass on dynamic MRI. Repair must incorporate obliteration of the peritoneal sac.

Obstetric perineal trauma disrupts both the perineal body and the external anal sphincter, combining the hernia with fecal incontinence. A colorectal surgeon is needed for concurrent sphincteroplasty.

primarySymptoms: A bulge or descent of the perineal skin between the vaginal opening and the anus, particularly noticeable on straining, Valsalva manoeuvre, or prolonged standing — the pathognomonic external sign of a large perineocele.
Incomplete bowel emptying and the need to strain excessively to defecate, caused by loss of the anorectal angle support provided by an intact perineal body.
Pelvic and perineal pressure or heaviness that worsens progressively through the day and with physical activity.
Obstructed defecation — a sense of functional blockage during defecation that may prompt digital reduction of the perineum to allow stool passage.
Perineal pain or discomfort, particularly after prolonged sitting or standing, related to stretching of the attenuated perineal body.
Dyspareunia or loss of vaginal calibre sensation during intercourse due to posterior wall laxity associated with perineal body disruption.
Fecal urgency or frank incontinence when concurrent sphincter injury is present alongside the perineal body defect.
Difficulty or discomfort when sitting on hard surfaces, often attributable to the exposed or prolapsed perineal tissue.
A visible perineal gap or increased anovaginal distance, which the patient may notice during personal hygiene.
Urinary symptoms including stress incontinence when the concurrent anterior compartment is also prolapsed, as is common in multi-compartment pelvic floor failure.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing that the perineal skin between the vaginal opening and the anus appears to bulge or descend when straining.
Progressive worsening of bowel emptying difficulty over months to years following a vaginal delivery with perineal tear or episiotomy.
A vague sense of perineal fullness that was not present before childbirth.
Increasing perineal laxity or a widened anovaginal distance noticed during personal hygiene.
emergencySigns: A large irreducible perineal mass with overlying skin ulceration or bleeding, indicating incarcerated or strangulated herniated bowel.
Acute inability to defecate or urinate due to a markedly prolapsed and incarcerated perineal hernia compressing adjacent structures.
Fever with a painful, tender perineal swelling suggesting abscess formation within the herniated contents.
Sudden onset of severe perineal pain with a visible mass at the perineum following heavy straining — requires urgent surgical assessment.

causes.cause: Obstetric perineal injury during vaginal delivery
causes.description: Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears and severe episiotomy extension directly disrupt the central fibromuscular tendon of the perineal body. Incomplete anatomical repair or failure of primary healing leaves a permanent structural defect through which pelvic contents can later herniate, particularly as supporting connective tissue ages and oestrogen declines.

causes.cause: Attenuation of perineal body connective tissue with ageing
causes.description: The perineal body is a highly collagen-rich structure that loses tensile strength with normal ageing. This age-related attenuation is greatly accelerated by oestrogen deficiency after menopause; women in their fifth to seventh decades experience progressive weakening even without gross obstetric tears, providing the mechanical backdrop for delayed perineocele onset years after delivery.

Repeated Valsalva manoeuvre from chronic constipation, heavy manual labour, or obesity creates persistent downward pressure across the pelvic floor. The perineal body, as the most distal anchor point, bears a disproportionate share of this pressure and gradually stretches and descends over years of cumulative mechanical stress.

causes.cause: Prior pelvic surgery altering compartment dynamics
causes.description: Hysterectomy, posterior colporrhaphy, or sacrocolpopexy can alter load distribution across the posterior compartment, redirecting stress toward the perineal body and predisposing to perineocele formation as a late complication, particularly when the perineal body was not reconstructed at the time of the original procedure.

Heritable defects in collagen metabolism — notably Ehlers–Danlos syndrome (hypermobility type) and Marfan syndrome — reduce the mechanical stiffness of all pelvic connective tissue, predisposing affected women to multi-compartment prolapse including perineocele at a younger age and with less obstetric provocation.

Pudendal nerve neuropathy acquired during prolonged second-stage labour or from chronic straining denervates the external anal sphincter and perineal muscles, leading to progressive pelvic floor descent and facilitating herniation through the structurally unsupported perineal body.

riskFactors.description: Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears, prolonged second-stage labour, and large-for-gestational-age infants all cause direct perineal body disruption; women with these delivery complications carry significantly higher risk of late perineocele.

Each vaginal delivery incrementally stresses the perineal body; cumulative obstetric loading with three or more vaginal deliveries substantially increases the lifetime risk of posterior compartment prolapse including perineocele.
Oestrogen decline accelerates collagen degradation in the perineal body and pelvic fascia; topical vaginal oestrogen can partially mitigate this but does not reverse established structural defects.
Habitual straining delivers cumulative mechanical insult to the perineal body; high-fibre diet, adequate hydration, and bowel management substantially reduce this modifiable risk.
Excess abdominal weight chronically elevates intra-abdominal pressure, accelerating descent of an already weakened perineal body; weight loss reduces symptom severity and progression risk.

riskFactors.factor: Prior posterior compartment surgery without perineal body reconstruction
riskFactors.description: Rectocele or enterocele repair that does not reconstruct the perineal body may resolve the fascial defect while leaving the perineal anchor weak, increasing the risk of subsequent perineocele.
Ehlers–Danlos syndrome and Marfan syndrome predispose to multi-compartment prolapse at younger ages through intrinsically deficient collagen and fascial integrity.
Occupations or sports requiring repeated heavy lifting or high-impact activity substantially increase cumulative perineal loading; ergonomic training and pelvic floor conditioning are protective.

affectedDemographics: Parous women, particularly those who sustained third- or fourth-degree perineal tears at delivery
Postmenopausal women in their fifth to seventh decades presenting with progressive bowel or perineal symptoms
Women with a prior history of posterior colporrhaphy who develop recurrent posterior bulge symptoms
Women with Ehlers–Danlos syndrome or other heritable connective-tissue disorders
Women with chronic constipation and prolonged straining history
Women presenting with combined fecal incontinence and posterior vaginal bulge after obstetric trauma
diagnosisOverview: Perineocele is frequently missed on routine pelvic examination because the perineal bulge occupies a lower position than the typical posterior vaginal wall and may not be detected with a standard Sims speculum positioned to assess the upper vagina. Diagnosis requires deliberate examination of the perineum itself, with the speculum removed, asking the patient to strain maximally: in a perineocele the perineal body descends visibly and the anovaginal distance widens beyond the normal threshold of approximately 3–4 cm. Dynamic defaecography — either fluoroscopic with barium or MRI-based — is the definitive diagnostic modality. It demonstrates herniation of pelvic contents through the perineal body during straining and allows quantification of perineal descent, distinguishing perineocele from rectocele (which occurs higher in the posterior vaginal canal) and enterocele (which arises from the apical compartment). MRI defaecography has the additional advantage of visualising the levator ani, perineal body integrity, and sphincter complex in a single examination. Anorectal manometry and endoanal ultrasound are added when fecal incontinence or sphincter disruption is clinically suspected, given the frequent coexistence with sphincter injury. POP-Q staging records all three compartments to guide comprehensive surgical planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects abnormal perineal descent and widened anovaginal distance at the perineum rather than the mid-vagina, differentiating perineocele from higher posterior wall defects.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician removes the speculum and asks you to bear down or cough while observing the perineum between the vaginal opening and the anus. The test takes 2–3 minutes and is mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises rectal and perineal dynamics in real time during defecation, confirming herniation through the perineal body and quantifying perineal descent relative to bony landmarks.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast paste is placed in the rectum and you sit on a commode while X-ray images are taken at rest, squeezing, and during defecation. The procedure takes about 20–30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides high-resolution imaging of all pelvic compartments, the perineal body, levator ani, and sphincter complex without radiation, used in surgical planning for complex or recurrent cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in an MRI scanner for 30–45 minutes and perform Valsalva manoeuvres on the radiologist's instruction. No contrast injection is needed for standard pelvic floor MRI.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the integrity of the external and internal anal sphincters for occult obstetric tears that frequently coexist with perineal body disruption.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small rotating ultrasound probe is gently inserted into the anal canal for approximately 10–15 minutes; it is mildly uncomfortable and provides immediate results.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies anal sphincter pressures and rectal sensory thresholds, guiding the surgical decision between perineorrhaphy alone and combined sphincteroplasty.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin probe with a small balloon is gently inserted into the rectum. You squeeze and relax on command while pressure readings are recorded; the test takes 20–30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardised quantification of all three vaginal compartments to document prolapse extent, guide operative planning, and allow outcome comparison.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician uses a speculum and measures the position of specific vaginal points in centimetres relative to the hymenal ring while you strain. The full examination takes about 10 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Management depends on symptom severity, the grade of perineal descent, and the presence of co-existing posterior compartment defects. Most mild perineoceles are managed conservatively with pelvic floor muscle training, bowel optimisation, and vaginal pessary support. Physiotherapist-directed pelvic floor rehabilitation can reduce perineal descent and improve bowel and continence symptoms in Grade 1–2 cases; a bulge pessary or ring pessary provides mechanical support for women who prefer to defer surgery or are medically unfit. Topical vaginal oestrogen is prescribed to all postmenopausal women to restore tissue quality before and after repair. Surgical correction — perineorrhaphy — is indicated when the perineal defect causes significant obstructed defecation, perineal pain, or fecal incontinence that has not responded to conservative measures, and when quality of life is substantially affected. The standard procedure is a posterior perineorrhaphy in which the attenuated perineal body is reconstructed by carefully approximating the disrupted perineal muscles and overlying fascia in anatomical layers using absorbable sutures. When a concurrent rectocele, enterocele, or external anal sphincter defect is present — as is common — a combined procedure addressing all pelvic compartments simultaneously produces significantly better functional outcomes than sequential staged repairs. Postoperative pelvic floor physiotherapy and ongoing bowel management are essential to sustain the repair long-term.

medicalTreatments.name: Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)
medicalTreatments.description: A structured physiotherapy programme targeting the levator ani, external anal sphincter, and perineal muscles. Regular Kegel exercises and functional pelvic floor training reduce perineal descent and improve bowel and continence symptoms. Programmes run 12–24 weeks with a minimum of 3 sets of 10 contractions daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves meaningful symptom reduction in approximately 30–40% of women with Grade 1–2 perineocele; evidence primarily extrapolated from posterior compartment prolapse trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical vaginal oestrogen
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose oestrogen cream or pessary applied to the vaginal vestibule and perineum restores collagen content and tissue resilience in postmenopausal women. Prescribed for at least 6–12 weeks before surgical repair to optimise healing and reduce postoperative infection risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves vaginal tissue quality in over 80% of postmenopausal women; associated with approximately 25–30% reduction in surgical wound complications in observational series.

medicalTreatments.name: Bowel management and biofeedback
medicalTreatments.description: A comprehensive programme of dietary fibre supplementation (25–30 g daily), adequate hydration, regular toileting routine, and, where anismus is identified, biofeedback retraining. Reduces straining-related mechanical stress on the perineal body and improves defecatory function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in bowel emptying in approximately 40–55% of women with mild perineocele and obstructed defecation when consistently applied.

medicalTreatments.name: Vaginal pessary
medicalTreatments.description: A supportive silicone device fitted vaginally to mechanically support the posterior compartment and perineal body. A ring or Gellhorn pessary can reduce perineal descent and improve symptoms in women who are awaiting surgery, have significant comorbidity, or prefer non-surgical management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides satisfactory symptom control in approximately 50–65% of women at 12 months; up to 40% discontinue owing to discomfort, expulsion, or difficulty with self-care.

surgicalOptions.name: Posterior perineorrhaphy
surgicalOptions.description: The definitive repair: the perineal body is reconstructed by identifying and reapproximating the disrupted perineal muscles (bulbospongiosus, transverse perinei) and overlying fascia in anatomical layers with absorbable sutures. The vaginal mucosa and perineal skin are closed, restoring the structural anchor of the posterior pelvic floor. It is often performed concurrently with posterior colporrhaphy for a combined posterior repair.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical restoration of the perineal body in approximately 85–95% of cases; subjective symptom improvement in 70–80% of women at one year.

surgicalOptions.name: Combined posterior colporrhaphy and perineorrhaphy
surgicalOptions.description: Simultaneous repair of a co-existing rectocele and perineocele through a single posterior vaginal incision, reconstructing both the rectovaginal fascia and the perineal body. This comprehensive approach is preferred when both defects are identified preoperatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Combined anatomical and symptomatic success rates of 78–92% at two years; lower recurrence rates than staged repairs.

surgicalOptions.name: Sphincteroplasty with perineorrhaphy
surgicalOptions.description: When endoanal ultrasound demonstrates a concurrent external anal sphincter defect causing fecal incontinence, an overlapping or end-to-end sphincteroplasty is performed at the same sitting as the perineorrhaphy. A colorectal surgeon typically performs the sphincter reconstruction while the urogynecologist addresses the fascial and vaginal components.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Continence improvement in 50–80% of women at one year; results decline over five years as sphincter muscle quality continues to change with age and neuropathy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Sensor-guided pelvic floor coordination training, particularly useful when paradoxical puborectalis contraction contributes to obstructed defecation alongside the structural perineal defect. Four to eight sessions with a pelvic floor physiotherapist.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tibial nerve stimulation
alternativeTreatments.description: Posterior tibial nerve stimulation can modulate pelvic floor neurological function and has been used as an adjunct for fecal urgency associated with perineal body weakness; evidence is limited to small series and it is not guideline-endorsed for perineocele specifically.

whySeeSpecialist: A urogynecologist or female pelvic medicine and reconstructive surgery specialist should be consulted whenever a woman with pelvic floor symptoms has an abnormal perineal descent or widened anovaginal distance on examination, when posterior compartment repair has already been performed without lasting symptom relief, or when defaecography reveals perineal herniation that the clinical examination did not capture. Colorectal surgical input is essential when fecal incontinence co-exists, since sphincteroplasty demands a separate technical skill set. Multi-disciplinary pelvic floor assessment is the most reliable path to an accurate diagnosis and appropriately comprehensive surgical plan.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a urogynecologist or FPMRS specialist with access to dynamic defaecography and pelvic floor MRI, and with experience in posterior and perineal compartment repairs. Confirm that the centre performs combined procedures with colorectal colleagues when sphincter injury is present. Ask specifically about the surgeon's familiarity with perineocele — because the condition is frequently misdiagnosed, this question reliably identifies practitioners with genuine pelvic floor subspecialty expertise.
preventionStrategies: Maintain good bowel habits with a high-fibre diet and adequate fluid intake to prevent straining.
Practise pelvic floor muscle exercises consistently from pregnancy onwards and throughout the postpartum period.
Ensure adequate repair of perineal tears at delivery; request consultant-level repair for third- or fourth-degree injuries.
Use topical vaginal oestrogen after menopause to preserve perineal connective-tissue integrity.
Avoid chronic heavy lifting without proper core and pelvic floor engagement.
Address persistent respiratory conditions causing chronic cough, which repetitively increases perineal pressure.
lifestyleModifications: Eat 25–30 g of dietary fibre daily and drink at least 1.5–2 litres of fluid to maintain soft, easily passed stools.
Use a footstool to adopt a squatting position on the toilet, which optimises the anorectal angle and reduces straining force on the perineum.
Perform daily pelvic floor contractions in multiple positions — lying, sitting, and standing — to build functional pelvic floor strength.
Avoid prolonged standing, heavy impact exercise, or excessive lifting during symptomatic flares.
Change sanitary products or check pessary status regularly to prevent erosion of vulnerable perineal skin.
Address any urge or fecal incontinence promptly with physiotherapy rather than tolerating it; early intervention prevents progressive deconditioning of the sphincter mechanism.

recommended: High-fibre foods: lentils, oats, pears, leafy vegetables, and wholegrains to normalise bowel transit and reduce straining
At least 1.5–2 litres of water and non-caffeinated fluids daily to soften stools
Psyllium husk or methylcellulose as a fibre supplement when dietary intake is insufficient
Probiotic-rich foods such as yoghurt and kefir to support gut regularity
avoid: Refined low-fibre foods such as white bread and processed snacks that contribute to constipation
Excess caffeine and alcohol, which have a dehydrating effect and can worsen constipation
Large irregular meals that disrupt regular bowel transit rhythm

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic exercise — walking, swimming, and cycling — is encouraged throughout management. High-impact activities including running, jump training, and heavy resistance exercise should be deferred until pelvic floor strength is established through supervised physiotherapy. Return-to-sport programmes after perineorrhaphy typically progress from light walking at 2 weeks to low-impact gym work at 6–8 weeks and full activity at 3 months. A pelvic floor physiotherapist should supervise the return-to-exercise plan, paying particular attention to loading the perineum incrementally.
dailyManagement: Perform 3 sets of 10–12 slow-twitch and fast-twitch pelvic floor contractions daily, ideally at the same time each day.
Follow a consistent toileting routine and take adequate time without hurrying; never force a bowel movement.
Maintain pessary hygiene if a pessary is being used; attend clinic review every 3–6 months.
Keep a symptom diary noting perineal bulge severity, bowel-emptying completeness, and any continence episodes.
Report worsening perineal pain, new bleeding, increasing bulge beyond the perineum, or difficulty urinating immediately.
prognosis: When perineocele is accurately diagnosed and appropriately treated, the prognosis is favourable. Conservative management with PFMT and bowel rehabilitation achieves acceptable symptom control in approximately 35–50% of women with Grade 1–2 defects, allowing surgery to be deferred or avoided. Surgical reconstruction of the perineal body provides anatomical restoration in 85–95% of cases and improves bowel evacuation and perineal symptoms in approximately 75–80% of women. The coexisting fecal incontinence component responds less predictably: sphincteroplasty achieves continence improvement in 50–80% of women initially, but the benefit tends to diminish over five to ten years as sphincter muscle undergoes further age-related change and pudendal neuropathy progresses. Recurrence after careful repair is estimated at 8–15% at five years, primarily in women who continue to strain excessively, gain weight, or fail to maintain pelvic floor conditioning. Multi-compartment repair in a single operation generally produces more durable outcomes than staged procedures.
recoveryTimeline: After posterior perineorrhaphy, most women are discharged within 24–48 hours. Perineal wound discomfort and swelling typically settle within 2–4 weeks. Light activity and non-strenuous walking resume at 2 weeks. Driving is usually permitted at 2–3 weeks. Sexual intercourse is deferred for 8 weeks to allow full perineal healing. Heavy lifting and high-impact exercise are avoided for 12 weeks. Wound assessment is performed at 6–8 weeks, with functional review at 3–6 months and one year.
complications: Perineal wound dehiscence or infection — superficial wound breakdown occurs in approximately 3–8% of cases; most resolve with wound care and antibiotics.
De novo or worsened dyspareunia — reported by 10–15% of women after perineorrhaphy, often related to over-tightening of the vaginal calibre during repair.
Persistent obstructed defecation — structural repair does not resolve a neuromuscular defecatory component, and up to 25% of women report ongoing evacuation difficulty postoperatively.
Recurrence of perineal prolapse — estimated at 8–15% at five years, particularly with resumed straining or progressive connective-tissue atrophy.

Fecal incontinence unmasking — rarely, successful restoration of the perineal body eliminates tissue support that was compensating for underlying sphincter weakness, unmasking previously subclinical incontinence.
Rectovaginal fistula — a rare but serious complication of posterior compartment surgery, occurring in less than 1% of cases, requiring staged repair.

supportResources.name: AUGS — Pelvic Organ Prolapse Patient Education
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient education materials on posterior compartment prolapse, surgical options, and pelvic floor rehabilitation from the American Urogynecologic Society.

supportResources.name: NHS — Pelvic organ prolapse: overview
supportResources.description: UK patient guide to recognising, investigating, and treating pelvic organ prolapse including posterior compartment defects.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Pelvic Floor Disorders
supportResources.description: Overview of pelvic floor conditions including prolapse and fecal incontinence from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

sources.title: Maher C et al. Surgical management of pelvic organ prolapse in women. Cochrane Database Syst Rev, 2013
ACOG Practice Bulletin 214 — Pelvic Organ Prolapse, 2019
Barber MD, Maher C. Epidemiology and outcome assessment of pelvic organ prolapse. Int Urogynecol J, 2013
Nichols DH, Randall CL. Posterior colporrhaphy and perineorrhaphy. In: Vaginal Surgery, 4th ed. Williams & Wilkins, 1996
Hagen S, Stark D. Conservative prevention and management of pelvic organ prolapse in women. Cochrane Database Syst Rev, 2011
Orejuela FJ et al. Perineal body anatomy. Int Urogynecol J, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Rectocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A rectocele bulges through the rectovaginal fascia into the mid-vaginal canal; a perineocele herniates through the perineal body below the anus, producing a perineal rather than mid-vaginal bulge.

confusedWithConditions.name: Enterocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An enterocele involves small bowel descending through the upper posterior vaginal vault near the apex; perineocele occurs at the lowest level of the posterior perineum and is distinguished by defaecography.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rectal prolapse
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rectal prolapse presents as a red cylindrical protrusion of full-thickness rectum through the anus; perineocele is an internal perineal hernia visible as a soft skin-covered bulge between the anus and the vagina.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pelvic muscle wasting
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pelvic muscle wasting is a generalised finding of levator ani atrophy contributing to prolapse; perineocele is a specific anatomical herniation at the perineal body level with distinct clinical and radiological features.

relatedConditions.name: Rectocele
relatedConditions.relevance: Rectocele and perineocele are the two main posterior compartment defects and most commonly coexist; distinguishing them preoperatively by defaecography determines whether rectovaginal fascial repair, perineal body reconstruction, or both are required.

relatedConditions.name: Enterocele
relatedConditions.relevance: Enterocele frequently accompanies perineocele, particularly in women who have had hysterectomy; combined posterior and apical repair produces better long-term outcomes than addressing each defect separately.

relatedConditions.name: Pelvic muscle wasting
relatedConditions.relevance: Levator ani atrophy and perineal muscle wasting are the neuromuscular substrate common to perineocele and other pelvic floor disorders, and are the target of physiotherapy and electrical stimulation.

relatedConditions.name: Cystocele
relatedConditions.relevance: Multi-compartment prolapse including anterior wall cystocele is common in women with perineocele; anterior repair should be planned concurrently to avoid worsening anterior prolapse after posterior reconstruction.

faqs.question: What is a perineocele?
faqs.answer: A perineocele is a herniation of pelvic contents — usually small bowel, fat, or peritoneum — through a weakened perineal body. The perineal body is the fibromuscular pyramid between the vaginal opening and the anus. A perineocele creates a bulge at the perineum rather than inside the vaginal canal and is distinct from a rectocele or enterocele.

faqs.question: How is a perineocele different from a rectocele?
faqs.answer: A rectocele is a bulge of the rectal wall into the mid-vaginal canal through a defect in the rectovaginal fascia. A perineocele occurs lower, at the perineal body, between the anus and the vaginal opening, often involving small bowel rather than the rectal wall. They are distinguished by defaecography or pelvic MRI.

faqs.question: How is perineocele diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a deliberate perineal examination with the patient straining, which reveals abnormal descent of the perineal skin. Confirmation is by dynamic defaecography or pelvic floor MRI, which shows herniation through the perineal body during Valsalva. Standard vaginal speculum examination often misses the defect.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of perineocele?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are a perineal bulge or skin descent between the vaginal opening and the anus, pelvic and perineal pressure that worsens through the day, obstructed defecation, incomplete bowel emptying, and perineal pain on sitting. Fecal urgency or incontinence occurs when there is concurrent sphincter injury.

faqs.question: Can a perineocele cause bowel problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Disruption of the perineal body alters the anorectal angle and reduces the structural support that normally aids defecation. This produces obstructed defecation — the need to strain excessively, a sensation of incomplete emptying, and sometimes the need to reduce the perineal bulge digitally to complete a bowel movement.

faqs.question: What causes perineocele?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is obstetric perineal trauma, particularly third- or fourth-degree perineal tears during vaginal delivery that are not fully repaired. Postmenopausal connective-tissue atrophy, chronic straining, obesity, and prior posterior pelvic surgery also contribute by weakening the perineal body over time.

faqs.question: Does perineocele need surgery?
faqs.answer: Not always. Grade 1–2 defects are often managed conservatively with pelvic floor exercises, bowel optimisation, and pessary support. Surgery — perineorrhaphy — is recommended when symptoms are significant, quality of life is impaired, and conservative measures have failed after 3–6 months of consistent effort.

faqs.question: What does perineorrhaphy involve?
faqs.answer: Perineorrhaphy reconstructs the perineal body by reapproximating the perineal muscles and fascia in anatomical layers using absorbable sutures, restoring the structural anchor of the posterior pelvic floor. It is usually combined with posterior colporrhaphy if a co-existing rectocele is also present.

faqs.question: Is perineocele common?
faqs.answer: Perineocele is considered underdiagnosed because it is frequently misclassified as a rectocele or enterocele on clinical examination. Studies using systematic defaecography suggest it may account for 5–15% of posterior-compartment prolapse cases referred for imaging evaluation.

faqs.question: Can perineocele cause fecal incontinence?
faqs.answer: Perineocele itself does not directly cause fecal incontinence, but the same obstetric injury that disrupts the perineal body often also tears the external anal sphincter. When both defects coexist, combined perineorrhaphy and sphincteroplasty are required for comprehensive repair.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after perineorrhaphy?
faqs.answer: Most women are discharged within 24–48 hours. Perineal discomfort settles over 2–4 weeks. Light activity resumes at 2 weeks, driving at 2–3 weeks, and sexual intercourse is deferred for 8 weeks. Heavy lifting and high-impact exercise are avoided for 3 months to allow full perineal healing.

faqs.question: Can I do pelvic floor exercises with a perineocele?
faqs.answer: Yes, and these exercises are a first-line conservative treatment. A pelvic floor physiotherapist can assess whether you are contracting the correct muscles and prescribe a structured programme targeting the levator ani, perineal, and sphincter muscles. Exercises should not cause pain; stop and seek guidance if they do.

faqs.question: Will a vaginal pessary help a perineocele?
faqs.answer: A ring or Gellhorn pessary can provide mechanical support for the posterior compartment and reduce perineal descent and symptom severity. Pessaries are a good option while awaiting surgery or for women with significant medical comorbidities. They require clinic fitting and 3–6-monthly review.

faqs.question: What happens if perineocele is not treated?
faqs.answer: Small stable defects may remain symptomatic but not worsen for years with lifestyle management. Larger defects tend to progress with menopause, weight gain, or persistent straining, producing worsening obstructed defecation, greater perineal descent, and an increased risk of mucosal ulceration or sphincter decompensation.

faqs.question: Is perineocele related to childbirth?
faqs.answer: Childbirth is the dominant cause. Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears during vaginal delivery directly disrupt the perineal body. Subsequent hormonal and age-related connective-tissue changes then permit herniation through the scarred or attenuated perineal body, often decades after the original delivery.

faqs.question: Can perineocele affect urination?
faqs.answer: Perineocele primarily affects bowel function, but large posterior prolapses can indirectly compress the posterior bladder base and contribute to urinary urgency or incomplete bladder emptying. Many women with perineocele also have anterior wall prolapse, which directly causes urinary symptoms and warrants separate assessment.

faqs.question: What specialist treats perineocele?
faqs.answer: A urogynecologist or female pelvic medicine and reconstructive surgery specialist is the primary clinician. When fecal incontinence or sphincter injury coexists, joint care with a colorectal surgeon experienced in sphincter repair and pelvic floor disorders is needed.

faqs.question: How successful is perineorrhaphy?
faqs.answer: Perineorrhaphy restores the perineal body anatomy in approximately 85–95% of cases. Subjective improvement in perineal symptoms and bowel evacuation is reported by 70–80% of women at one year. Recurrence over five years occurs in 8–15% of women, most often in those with persistent straining or progressive collagen loss.

faqs.question: What imaging is used to diagnose perineocele?
faqs.answer: Dynamic defaecography — either fluoroscopic or MRI-based — is the definitive imaging test. It shows herniation through the perineal body during straining and quantifies perineal descent. MRI defaecography is preferred when concurrent evaluation of the levator ani and sphincter is needed for surgical planning.

faqs.question: Can perineocele recur after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in approximately 8–15% of women within five years after perineorrhaphy. Risk is higher when predisposing factors persist: chronic constipation and straining, obesity, postmenopausal tissue atrophy without oestrogen therapy, or failure to maintain pelvic floor muscle conditioning postoperatively.

metaDescription: A perineocele is a perineal body hernia causing pelvic pressure and obstructed defecation. Learn symptoms, diagnosis by MRI defaecography, and surgical repair.
